facts.fact: Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm (Tortoise and Hare) uses two pointers moving at different speeds: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. Time complexity O(n), space complexity O(1).
Sliding window converts O(n²) or O(n³) nested loop problems into O(n) by maintaining a contiguous window that expands and contracts based on validity conditions.
Fixed-size sliding window requires O(1) space (only a few variables) and O(n) time—one complete pass through the array regardless of window size.
Variable-size sliding window uses a hash set or hash map to track window contents, expanding the right pointer when the condition is valid and contracting the left pointer when invalid.
Monotonic stack solves 'next greater/smaller element' and range query problems in O(n) time: each element is pushed once and popped once, despite nested loop structure.
Largest Rectangle in Histogram can be solved with a monotonic stack in O(n) time and O(n) space: when a bar is popped, its span (left and right boundaries) is fully determined.

Prefix sums enable O(1) range sum queries after O(n) preprocessing: sum(l, r) = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. For 2D arrays, use inclusion-exclusion: sum(x1,y1,x2,y2) = prefix[x2][y2] - prefix[x1-1][y2] - prefix[x2][y1-1] + prefix[x1-1][y1-1].
For Q range sum queries on an array of size N: prefix sum achieves O(n + q) vs naive O(q*n). For 2D prefix sums, preprocessing is O(N*M) and each query is O(1).
Happy Number problem detects cycles using Floyd's algorithm: repeated squaring of digits forms a sequence; if it reaches 1, the number is happy; if it cycles without reaching 1, it is not happy.

Two-pointer vs sliding window distinction: two pointers move toward each other or independently (pair problems, sorted arrays); sliding window maintains a contiguous region that expands/contracts (subarray problems).
Linked List Cycle II: after detecting a cycle with fast/slow pointers, reset one pointer to head and advance both by 1 step; the meeting point is the start of the cycle.
Floyd's cycle finding operates in O(λ + μ) operations where μ is steps to cycle start and λ is cycle length; space complexity is O(1).

examples.example: Container With Most Water: Two pointers start at opposite ends of array. Move the pointer pointing to the shorter bar inward. Time O(n), space O(1). Real company interview frequency: Facebook, Google, Amazon.
Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters: Variable sliding window with hash map. Expand right, contract left when duplicate found. LeetCode #3, common in FAANG interviews.
Find All Anagrams in a String: Fixed-size sliding window matching character frequency. O(n) time, O(1) space (26 letters max). LeetCode #438.
Next Greater Element: Monotonic decreasing stack iterates right-to-left, popping smaller elements and recording the first larger one. O(n) time despite nested structure.
Largest Rectangle in Histogram: Monotonic increasing stack tracks bar indices. When a smaller bar is found, pop and calculate area. O(n) time, O(n) space. Core problem in interview loops.
Range Sum Query (NumArray): 1D prefix sums precompute cumulative sums in O(n). Each query answered in O(1). LeetCode #303, foundational for interview questions.
Trapping Rain Water: Two-pointer approach with left/right tracking of max heights. O(n) time, O(1) space. LeetCode #42, asked at Google, Microsoft.
Find Duplicate in Array: Floyd's cycle detection treats array as linked list (array[i] is next pointer). O(n) time, O(1) space. LeetCode #287, asked at Apple, Bloomberg.
Happy Number: Floyd's fast/slow pointers on digit square sum sequence. O(1) space vs O(log n) for hash set. LeetCode #202.
2D Range Sum Query: 2D prefix sum preprocessing O(n*m), then O(1) per query using 4-point inclusion-exclusion formula. LeetCode #304.

analogies.analogy: Sliding window is like a train window moving along a track: the window size is fixed or adjusts based on track conditions (validity). You only see what's in the window, not the entire landscape.
Monotonic stack is like stacking books by height: when you add a taller book, shorter books on top are displaced. Reading the book that gets displaced tells you the next taller book you encountered.
Fast/slow pointers are like two runners on a circular track: the fast runner will eventually lap the slow runner, revealing the cycle. If there's no cycle, the fast runner exits the track first.

Prefix sums are like cumulative wealth: instead of recounting coins from year 0 every time, you store cumulative totals. To find wealth gained from year 5 to 10, subtract year 4's total from year 10's.
Two-pointer for sorted arrays is like a seesaw: heavier people (larger values) on one side, lighter on the other. Adjust from the heavier end to balance (find pair, optimize, etc.).

key_numbers.context: Space complexity of Floyd's cycle detection algorithm using only two pointers, regardless of input size.
Time complexity of Floyd's cycle detection; each element visited at most twice (fast pointer makes 2n moves).
Sliding window optimization: converts nested loop brute force to single pass algorithm.
Typical time limit in competitive programming; O(n) passes 10^8 operations; O(n log n) passes 10^7 operations.
Floyd's complexity breakdown: λ = cycle length, μ = steps to cycle start. For happy numbers, λ + μ ≈ O(log n).
Prefix sum total complexity: O(n) preprocessing + O(1) per q queries vs O(q*n) for naive approach.
2D prefix sum query time after O(n*m) preprocessing, using 4-cell inclusion-exclusion formula.
Standard fast/slow pointer ratio (fast moves 2 steps, slow moves 1) guarantees they meet if cycle exists.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Sliding window and two pointers are the same technique.
common_misconceptions.reality: Sliding window maintains a contiguous valid region that expands/contracts; two pointers move independently or toward each other. Use sliding window for subarrays, two pointers for pair problems in sorted arrays.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Monotonic stack requires nested loops and is therefore O(n²).
common_misconceptions.reality: Despite visual nested loop structure, each element is pushed and popped exactly once, giving O(n) total operations.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Floyd's cycle detection only works for linked lists.
common_misconceptions.reality: Floyd's algorithm applies to any function that generates a sequence: arrays (as linked lists via index), digit transformations (happy number), or any repeating sequence.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prefix sums require O(n) space for the prefix array.
common_misconceptions.reality: Space is required, but queries become O(1) instead of O(n). Trade-off: O(n) space + O(1) query time vs O(1) space + O(n) query time. For multiple queries, prefix sums are better.

common_misconceptions.misconception: After detecting a cycle in Floyd's algorithm, you need to search separately to find the cycle start.
common_misconceptions.reality: Once pointers meet inside the cycle, reset one to head and advance both by 1 step at a time. Their meeting point IS the cycle start.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Variable sliding window always requires a hash map.
common_misconceptions.reality: Hash map or set is common for character tracking, but variable window can also use counters, arrays (for fixed alphabet), or other tracking structures.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Pattern recognition happens at problem statement reading; pattern selection is immediate.
common_misconceptions.reality: Recognizing which of 6 patterns applies takes deliberate practice and explicit analysis of constraints: size, sorted, substring vs subarray, pair vs range, uniqueness requirements.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prefix sums only work for addition.
common_misconceptions.reality: Prefix sums can be adapted for XOR, GCD, min/max, and other associative operations with proper preprocessing and query formulas.
